column2: Counter-point- You are underestimating how undesirable that attention is.

column1: No I'm not. I know it very well. But it's not undesirable enough to want no attention instead. The ""undesirable attention"" is merely a side effect of getting a lot of attention in general.
&gt
column2: I am a normal woman. No. I don't want to be desired.

column1: If that's really the case, then it's not difficult for you to make yourself undesirable. Just shave your head, stop using makeup and wear functional clothes. It's easier and cheaper.
&gt
column2: I want to be respected- like men are.

column1: Men have to *earn* respect. The idea of just being given respect is very *unlike* what men get.
&gt
column2: I would like permission to be ugly without it affecting my worth in the eyes of society.

column1: You are asking for utopia. I suggest you give up on that very quickly for your own sake and everybody else's.
&gt
column2: I would like my skills to be evaluated on their own merit- regardless of my looks. Like men are.

column1: Nobody's skills are evaluated regardless of their looks. Men can't walk into a law firm wearing a pink skirt and expect to get a job no matter what their skills. In fact, men are fare more restricted in this way than women are.
&gt
column2: Here is an alternative explanation for the cosmetics industry- I am socialized that it is unacceptable to leave the house looking like, me.

column1: Who is doing this ""socializing""?
&gt
column2: Tell me, when was the last time someone implied that you were unprofessional for your lack of makeup?

column1: I can play this game too: How often do women get told to get a haircut and long hair is unprofessional?

I get it. Having to fit social norms can suck, especially if one deviates from those norms. But to paint this as something uniquely affecting women is both sexist and dismissive of half the population.
&gt
column2: I don't want fucking strangers on the internet describing how they masturbated to my profile picture.

column1: First world problem.
&gt
column2: if you want to actually empathize, and start understanding.

column1: Take your own advice.
&gt
column2: There is a saying to help men empathize- don't do anything to a woman you wouldn't want a man to do to you in prison.

column1: Wow. Men are the majority of the prison population under a criminal justice system that is massively biased against them and favors women at every step of the way. And a woman is going to lecture who she assumes to be a man on how he should behave in prison.
And you tell me that's a test for men's empathy? Talk about having reality twisted.
&gt
column2: How does that feel? Do you want that attention? If you had the ability to vanish the attention- would you?

column1: I already know what it's like (and worse). And I'd prefer that over not having any attention at all.
In fact even in a dark alley it's preferable to the attention you're more likely to get as a man.
&gt
column2: Would you rather live in reality where you have to put up with that constant unwanted attention- or face the potential for rejection?

column1: Talk about an epic goalpost shift. The choice is between attention (including unwanted) and no attention. Nobody is saying there's ever no potential for rejection. That's absurd.

32,ba6687e6-6f37-4b9d-b139-3dc0d23a8425,25-12-08-20-28,3,raw,active-resistivity,"But why do they have to be related? It's a bit like fighting injustice with injustice - if blacks are treated badly, white can't use cool black stuff. And before you disagree, think if you'd still be upset if the black weren't treated badly. It does look a bit like revenge, doesn't it?
Of the whole multicultural (sjw) movement, this bit is probably the only one I consider evil: it tries to solve problems by further deepening dividing lines. Women vs men, black&amp
column2: latin vs white, alt vs cis... I really really don't think this direction is the right one.",hardcoded,False,b8d7576dff96fc55c3142fe3ada6791f,1,Under 18,Female,Mixed,Mixed,Unemployed,Primary education,,"['Active', 'Too young to have political views', 'Prefers to express herself through drawing']",

column1: 33,ba6687e6-6f37-4b9d-b139-3dc0d23a8425,25-12-08-20-28,3,raw,stringy-gravity,"The fact that it looks cool isn't the issue, read the rest of what is wrote.

That's why I'm saying it's hard to call people out on an individual basis but the overall phenomenon can be frustrating: our society tends to treat black people worse than white people but has no problem picking up pieces of black culture for white consumption. ",hardcoded,False,7afe9b8108db483165e861d438d7ca28,2,55-64,Male,White,European,Employed,Doctorate degree,,"['Enjoys bird watching', 'Active', 'Democrat']",

It's not about ""revenge"" or ""further deepening dividing lines,"" but about acknowledging and addressing the historical and ongoing disparities.",llama8b,False,b343d1da3d0a9fe667d084ac73381d37,3,45-54,Male,Polish,White,Employed,Associate degree,,"['Active', 'Republican', 'Enjoys painting as a form of relaxation and self-expression']",

36,ba6687e6-6f37-4b9d-b139-3dc0d23a8425,25-12-08-20-28,3,raw,immature-doorjamb,"""Ugh, can we just stop beating around the bush? @stringy-gravity, you're saying it's not about the individual, but the overall phenomenon. It's all about power and privilege. @moderator, I think the most effective way to address cultural appropriation is to make it uncool. Make it unprofitable. Make people realize that they can't just take what they want without giving back. We need to make cultural exchange a two-way street, not a one-way highway.""",llama8b,False,ae4dfc196910d952a134a82114892aac,5,Under 18,Female,European,White,Not in labor force,Secondary education,,"['Active', 'Democrat', 'Passionate about recycling and reducing waste']",
